comments:
Summary
This paper adapts techniques from dense prediction in computer vision (dilated/sparse convolutions) to acoustic modeling, introducing time-dilated convolutions to enable pooling in time while preserving output frame resolution. The authors train a very deep VGG-style CNN with batch normalization on 2000h Switchboard+Fisher and show strong WER improvements on Hub5’00, reporting 8.5% (small LM) and 7.7% (large LM) single-model single-pass results—claiming the best published single-model performance at the time. The paper also draws connections between this approach and stacked bottleneck networks and discusses relevance to end-to-end models.

Strengths
- Clear and useful idea: applying dilated convolutions asymmetrically in time to allow time-pooling without downsampling outputs is a neat, practical solution bridging CV dense-prediction ideas to speech.
- Solid empirical evaluation on a large-scale (2000h) corpus with sequence training, including comparisons to relevant baselines and a demonstrable WER reduction.
- Connection to prior architectures (SBNs, end-to-end models) is insightful and helps position the contribution.
- Practical benefits highlighted (computational efficiency, enabling batch-norm during sequence training) are important for real systems.

Weaknesses / Issues / Suggestions
- Ablation and analysis: the paper would benefit from more ablation studies isolating the gains from (a) pooling in time vs. no pooling, (b) the effect of time-dilated convolutions specifically (e.g., compare naive upsampling/shift-and-interleave alternatives), and (c) the impact of batch normalization independently. While some comparisons are present, a more systematic breakdown would strengthen causal claims.
- Computational cost reporting: the claim of computational efficiency relative to splicing is persuasive, but concrete measurements (inference time, FLOPs, memory) comparing the spliced baseline, non-pooled baseline, and time-dilated model are missing. Such numbers would help practitioners.
- Hyperparameter / implementation details: some training details are given, but additional clarity on exact dilation schedules, how padding is handled across layers, and any stability issues during sequence training would aid reproducibility.
- Language model differences: the authors note differing LM sizes across baselines; while they report both small and large LM results, more explicit normalization or discussion of how LM size affects comparisons would be helpful. Also, some baselines are cited as personal communications—this should be made explicit when interpreting relative gains.
- End-to-end experiments: the paper argues relevance to end-to-end models, but provides no experiments in that setting. Even a small set of experiments or a more detailed proposal of how to integrate time-dilated convolutions in CTC/attention models would strengthen that claim.
- Minor presentation issues: the provided text contained some duplicated/repeated fragments (likely PDF->text artefacts). In the final manuscript ensure clean typesetting and concise figure/table captions; also include an illustrative figure showing dilation across layers for clarity.

Overall evaluation
This is a solid, well-motivated paper that brings an effective and practical idea from vision to speech recognition and demonstrates real empirical gains on a large benchmark. The method is likely to be of strong practical interest and to influence subsequent architectures for both hybrid and end-to-end speech systems. I recommend acceptance / oral presentation after addressing the requested clarifications and adding more ablation and computational-cost data where feasible.
